title: Using Artificial Intelligence for Fraud Risk Assessment: Evidence from Novice and Experienced Auditors

study_design: To test our research questions, we first conduct two experiments in which practicing auditors assess fraud risk.
Our first experiment examines the judgments of experienced internal auditors using a 1x2 between-participants design in which we manipulate whether participants have access to GenAI.
Our second experiment examines whether these results extend to novice auditors.
We conduct a third experiment to explore how fully automated GenAI (i.e., without intervention from human auditors) performs fraud risk identification.
Both experiments 1 and 2 employ a 1x2 between-participants design to examine the effects of GenAI support on fraud risk assessments.
Using this modified case, we run three additional experiments using 485 novice external auditors, 73 experienced internal auditors, and two types of GenAI models (full and reasoning).
task: Participants review background information about the audit client, information about the industry and macroeconomic trends, details about sales and receivables, and preliminary analytical procedures before being asked to identify and list any fraud risk factors for the engagement.

In the scenario, the audit client experienced a sharp increase in days' sales outstanding after shifting sales responsibilities to third-party distributors and incentivizing those distributors to boost sales near year-end.
The scenario is indicative of revenue fraud, in particular the possibility that the audit client engaged in channel stuffing through third-party distributors.
Participants in the experimental condition had access to a GPT-4o-powered chatbot, preloaded with all case information, to assist in their assessments.
In the GenAI Condition, participants were given access to a GPT-4o-powered chatbot designed to provide case-specific assistance.
The chatbot could answer detailed questions about the case and offer suggestions for potential fraud risks.
In the Control Condition, participants performed the fraud risk identification task without any technological assistance.
To further explore the bounds of GenAI use in auditing, in additional analysis we examine a second task: the adjustment of audit plans to address identified fraud risks.
Participants are directly informed of the identified fraud risks, thus ensuring that all participants understand the elevated fraud risk.

Participants are also provided with the prior-year audit plan, including budgeted hours for certain procedures along with specific test details (e.g., in the prior year, cash receipts and sales returns tests were performed through February 15).
Participants are then asked to modify the audit plan for the current year.
population: The first experiment involved 79 experienced internal auditors, selected to evaluate how GenAI influences fraud risk judgments among individuals with established task-specific schemas.
These individuals completed the task via a Qualtrics link during training sessions in June and July 2024.
All participants were working in multinational companies at the time and had received training on general AI knowledge.

As detailed in Table 1, these participants had an average of 12 years of experience as assurance professionals, making them highly experienced professionals appropriate for assessing the impact of GenAI in a realistic setting.
The second experiment focused on 463 novice auditors, who completed the task via a Qualtrics link during their intern training at a Big 4 firm in June 2024.
As noted in Table 1, these individuals have approximately one month of assurance experience.
We note that participants in both experience pools report varied experience with GenAI in both their personal and professional life.
The majority of novices never use GenAI at work, consistent with many firm policies that GenAI tools not be used on assurance engagements.
However, the majority of both novice and experienced assurance professionals believe that GenAI can have positive effects on audit quality and audit efficiency.
Overall, evidence from 1,100 practicing auditors across six experiments provide important evidence on audit tasks that benefit from GenAI—and those that do not.
arms: Participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions—a GenAI condition or a control condition.
In the GenAI Condition, participants were given access to a GPT-4o-powered chatbot designed to provide case-specific assistance.
In the Control Condition, participants performed the fraud risk identification task without any technological assistance.
Participants in the Control Condition had access to all the same information as participants in the GenAI Condition.
Seventy-nine auditors, averaging 12 years of assurance experience, completed the study.
463 external audit interns from a Big 4 audit firm completed a similar experimental task as in Experiment 1.
Using that prompt, we provided the same audit scenario used in our two experiments to five fully automated GenAI models.

One of these models used a "reasoning" model released by ChatGPT (ChatGPT-o1-preview), two of these models utilized the "full" GenAI engines provided by leading GenAI providers (ChatGPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet), and two of these models utilized the "smaller" models provided by those providers (ChatGPT-4o mini and Claude 3.5 Haiku).
Each GenAI model completed 30 iterations of the case.
outcome_definitions: We measure five outcomes of the fraud risk identification task to capture audit effectiveness and efficiency.

Consistent with prior literature, we first measure the number of unique fraud risks identified by participants (Carpenter 2007; Lynch et al. 2009; Trotman et al. 2009; Chen et al. 2015; McAllister et al. 2021).

Next, using relevance scores validated by 10 experts with an average of 23 years of audit experience, we measure the number of highly relevant risks identified, the number of irrelevant risks identified, and the average relevance of all risks identified.
Finally, we measure the time participants spent on the task to assess whether GenAI improves the efficiency of auditors' fraud risk identification.
Total Number of Unique Risks is a count variable that measures the overall volume of risks listed by participants.
This measure is meant to capture each participant's ability to generate fraud risk ideas.
Number of Highly Relevant Risks is the number of risks identified by each participant that was within a category rated as highly relevant.
Number of Irrelevant Risks captures the number of risks identified by each participant that was within a category rated as irrelevant to the case at hand.

Average Risk Relevance is a weighted relevance score that provides an aggregate measure of the quality of identified risks, wherein irrelevant risks are given a value of 1 up to highly relevant risks, which are given a value of 4.
Finally, we measure Task Efficiency, equal to the amount of time in seconds participants spent completing the fraud risk assessment task.
statistical_results: Specifically, results from Experiment 1 show that experienced auditors with access to GenAI identified an average of 8.76 unique fraud risks, compared to 4.74 for those without GenAI (t = 5.20, p < 0.01).
From Experiment 2, we find that novice auditors with access to GenAI identified 6.41 unique risks on average, compared to 3.14 for those without GenAI (t = 16.55, p < 0.01).
Taken together, access to GenAI approximately doubled the volume of risks identified by both experienced and novice auditors.

We find that novice auditors with GenAI assistance not only identified more risks than novice auditors without GenAI but also more than experienced auditors working without GenAI (t = 3.92, p < 0.01).
Novice auditors with GenAI completed the task in 595.15 seconds, on average, versus 769.46 seconds without GenAI (t = 2.51, p = 0.01), indicating a significant efficiency improvement for novices.
Experienced auditors with GenAI completed the task in 1,349.78 seconds, compared to 1,908.79 seconds without GenAI (t = 1.38, p = 0.09, one-tailed).
In Experiment 1, experienced auditors with GenAI identified an average of 6.39 highly relevant risks, compared to 3.00 without GenAI (t = 5.54, p < 0.01).
Similarly, in Experiment 2, novice auditors with GenAI identified 4.69 highly relevant risks, compared to 2.06 without GenAI (t = 14.86, p < 0.01).

Further, as shown in Panel B, novice auditors with access to GenAI identify significantly more highly relevant fraud risks than their experienced auditor counterparts without access to GenAI (t = 4.48, p < 0.01).
For experienced auditors, the number of irrelevant risks with GenAI (0.93) is statistically indistinguishable from the number without GenAI (1.03; t = 0.43, p = 0.67).
Similarly, novice auditors with GenAI identify 0.73 irrelevant risks, versus 0.64 without GenAI (t = 1.09, p = 0.28).
Novice auditors' average risk relevance scores increase from 3.21 without GenAI to 3.43 with GenAI (t = 3.22, p < 0.01).
Experienced auditors' scores increase from 3.05 without GenAI to 3.45 with GenAI (t = 2.64, p = 0.01).

As shown in Panel B, access to GenAI fully levels the playing field across novice and experienced auditors as shown by statistically indistinguishable relevance scores across the two groups with access to GenAI (t = 0.23, p = 0.82).
Further, novices with access to GenAI generate risks that on average are statistically more relevant than experienced auditors without access to GenAI (t = 3.45, p<0.01).
The ChatGPT-o1-preview identified nearly four times as many total risks and highly relevant risks as experienced professionals who did not have access to GenAI.
We find that novices significantly increase their pass rate from 50 percent to 67 percent when given access to GenAI (p<0.01).
Experienced auditors experience a similar increase from 71 percent to 93 percent once given access to GenAI (p=0.01).
The pass rate of 67 percent for novices with GenAI is statistically indistinguishable from the pass rate of 71 percent for experienced auditors without AI (p=0.62).
On average, 98 percent of standalone GenAI tools pass using this most stringent measure.
Across both human participant pools, we find no consistent evidence that GenAI access influences auditors' sensitivity to fraud risk in their planning judgments.

Specifically, auditors with GenAI access did not plan significantly different hours for computer-assisted auditing techniques, interviews, "new" procedures, or total hours than auditors without access to GenAI.
Further, GenAI access did not lead to significant changes in the testing window for either cash receipts or sales returns—both critical for detecting fictitious sales transactions.
We also find no evidence that auditors with GenAI access change their testing of populations with higher ex-ante risk of fraud relative to auditors without access to GenAI.
Finally, we find no consistent evidence that access to GenAI affected the amount of time that either participant group took to complete the planning task (p>0.05).
In this regression, GenAI is positive and highly significant (t = 21.20, p<0.01), indicating that the use of GenAI results in responses that use more similar language.
We continue to find a significant effect of GenAI access on the number of highly relevant risks when we control for experienced auditors' years of work experience (p<0.01, untabulated).
In untabulated analysis, we find similar inferences if we look at the combined frequency of less-relevant and irrelevant risks (p=0.33 for novice comparisons; p=0.67 for experienced comparisons).

We find no evidence that participants' use of GenAI in their personal life moderates the observed effect of access to AI on any of our dependent variables (p > 0.05 on interaction term between this indicator variable and GenAI Condition in all regressions).
Additionally, novice auditors with GenAI completed the task 23 percent faster, demonstrating GenAI's impact on efficiency for novices.
ambiguity_or_caveats: One potential concern is that because the case is based on a published study, GenAI already "knew the answer" to the case and thus any comparison between treatment and control conditions lacks tension.
However, it is important to note that the large language models we used do not "look for" answers in their training data.
Instead, the training data is used to predict the next digit in its response.

Even if that were not the case, multiple results reject the notion that GenAI already knew the answer (e.g., significant variation exists between responses from within and between GenAI tools; we fail to find a significant effect of GenAI use on audit planning judgments, etc.).

In addition, at the time we conducted the study, we followed the methodology used by the New York Times in their lawsuit of OpenAI to test whether the experimental instrument we used was part of the public domain on which the chatbot was trained.
These tests revealed that the chatbot did not accurately predict the wording of the experimental instrument, suggesting it was not part of the public domain on which the chatbot was trained.
The tasks differed slightly across experiments because participants for experiment 1 were internal auditors.
This required minor modifications to the wording of the case to fit an external audit setting.

For example, participants are given information about "an audit engagement for which you are a team member" (novices) or "an internal audit engagement on which you will act as an internal audit manager" (experienced).
Importantly, the fundamental narrative, financial statement data, and prompt to identify fraud risks was identical across treatments.
These adaptations should have had no effect on the risks that should be identified.
While these slight differences complicate comparisons we make across experiments, they have no bearing on comparisons made within each experiment.

Further, we note that the magnitude of the GenAI benefit (i.e., doubling the number of identified fraud risks) is similar in each experiment, which suggests the two participant pools approach the task in a similar manner.
As noted above, human auditors in our experiments had access to a GPT-4o-powered chatbot and did not have access to more advanced reasoning models.
Thus, our comparisons of the fully-automated reasoning model with human auditors with AI should be interpreted with caution.
We remind readers to interpret these comparisons with caution, as there are slight wording differences between cases, previously described, that may contribute to the results.
That said, we believe it is unlikely that these slight differences would interact with the independent variable across the two experiments.
Nevertheless, readers should interpret these comparisons as exploratory in nature.
Bearing in mind the overarching limitation that we examine the specific context of fraud risk assessment, this research makes three primary contributions to the literature.
First, our experiments use a simplified audit engagement scenario and focus on the fraud setting.
GenAI's performance may differ across a broader range of contexts, engagement characteristics, volume of data required for analysis, and the novelty of seeded fraud.

Future research could examine whether GenAI's benefits persist when auditors examine novel versus generic fraud, work under varying task constraints, face more ambiguous client scenarios, or require analysis of a larger amount of data.
For instance, we did not have participants both identify fraud risks (our first three experiments) and plan the audit response to those fraud risks (experiments four through six).

Because prior research shows that auditors who identify more fraud schemes also identify more audit procedures that target the fraud (Simon 2012), it would be interesting to examine such a setting using GenAI.

It would also be useful to investigate how GenAI interacts with other factors, such as professional skepticism, team-level dynamics, institutional incentives, or the amount of response hallucinations.
We also do not examine the precise mechanisms giving rise to the differences we observe.
Second, as acknowledged earlier, slight differences across our experiments with novice and experienced auditors complicate direct comparisons across those two groups.
We report exploratory comparisons to provide initial insight on the extent to which GenAI bridges the expertise gap.
However, we encourage future research that more fully examines GenAI's ability to bridge the expertise gap in audit settings.
We made conscious changes to the case so that the firm's developed prompt would not be overly specific to the fraud case in which it would be tested.
This choice biases against us finding effectiveness using fully automated GenAI.
The innovation team noted that this was unrealistic for what happened in practice and a prompt would have to be designed to handle a more robust setting.
We encourage future research, likely using the design science research methodology (Myers et al. 2025), to study what would have to be done to fully incorporate GenAI usage in practice.
None of the same individuals from the risk assessment experiments participated in the risk response experiments.
We note that experienced auditors spent more time on the task than junior auditors.
Potential reasons for this difference include, but are not limited to, overreliance on GenAI among novices, greater levels of due diligence by experienced auditors, etc.
It is possible that human participants moderated GenAI's advice to the detriment of final planning decisions.
Results are mixed.
When examining total budgeted hours across a list of procedures, ChatGPT 4o (Claude Sonnet) suggested an average of 70 hours (188 hours), while o1-preview (o1-Pro) averaged 114 hours (81 hours).
This varied significantly from experienced auditors, who averaged 70 budgeted hours both with and without GenAI access.
We also observe wide variation in how GenAI models allocate hours to risk-focused procedures.

For instance, the number of days post-fiscal-year-end subject to cash receipts testing ranges from a low of 58 days (ChatGPT 4o) to a high of 91 days (o1-preview), relative to experienced auditors who averaged 79 and 82 days with and without access to GenAI, respectively.

Similarly, the percentage of tested transactions drawn from the highest-risk population ranged from 19 percent (ChatGPT 4o) to 43 percent (Claude Sonnet), relative to experienced auditors who averaged 27 and 28 percent with and without access to GenAI, respectively.
Overall, standalone GenAI models did not consistently focus planned audit procedures on risk in a way that was clearly better or worse than human participants.
Thus, while our main experiments provide evidence that GenAI excels at fraud risk identification, the same cannot be said for nuanced audit planning in response to those risks.
Such integration does not come without potential costs.

For example, integrating GenAI into existing audit workflows may present challenges in terms of 1) ensuring compliance with auditing standards that address the use of AI in the audit to varying degrees; 2) addressing ethical considerations, particularly concerns about GenAI potentially reducing the need for human auditors; 3) providing adequate training for audit professionals to effectively leverage AI technologies without over relying on GenAI to the detriment of skill development; and 4) complying with data privacy requirements using client data.

notes: This paper reports six experiments (two main experiments on fraud risk identification with experienced and novice auditors, one experiment on fully automated GenAI models, and three additional experiments on audit planning adjustments). The study involves 1,100 practicing auditors total. The main finding is that GenAI significantly improves fraud risk identification for both experienced and novice auditors, with novice auditors using GenAI outperforming experienced auditors without GenAI. However, GenAI does not provide clear benefits for audit planning adjustments in response to identified fraud risks. The paper also finds that fully automated GenAI models generally outperform human auditors with GenAI assistance on the fraud risk identification task.
